title: "CNA / vNIC Adapter Firmware Drift (Cisco UCS)"

# UC-19.1.32 · CNA / vNIC Adapter Firmware Drift (Cisco UCS)

> **Criticality:** Medium &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Configuration &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We make sure the little network cards on each server stay on the same software version your team has approved, slot by slot, so a half-updated card after a repair or upgrade does not quietly break fast storage and cluster networking later on.*

---

## Description

Flags Cisco UCSM-managed adaptorUnit and firmwareRunning rows where the on-card running or startup VIC, mLOM, mezzanine, or PCIe storage-adapter image does not match the model-keyed approved_fw string in the customer’s ucs_adapter_fw_baseline lookup, using the classId field on cisco:ucs:inventory instead of a fictional object_type=adapter filter, so you see per-slot drift before an OS upgrade, partial RMA, or phased bundle refresh leaves a CNA’s firmware behind the approved infrastructure bundle for FCoE, NVMe-oF, or RoCE-v2 paths.

## Value

When one slot still carries factory or last-quarter firmware, you get intermittent data-path faults that show up as driver panics, lost paths, or guest NIC resets after a routine OS maintenance cycle—hard to blame without a per-adapter, per-baseline line of evidence. Tight, model-keyed policy here lets you queue adapter activation before a PSIRT or VMware/RHEL qualification meeting, time RMAs with a bundle backplane, and show auditors that the converged-adapter line item was governed separately from the server BIOS and CIMC roll-ups, without mixing that story into a broad Intersight bundle comparison or a full HCL stack review.

## Implementation

Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, place `lookups/ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv` and optional `ucs_maintenance_windows.csv` in a private app, enable UCSM inventory for **adaptorUnit** and **firmwareRunning** MO classes, paste the `spl` into a scheduled report (daily or weekly) and an alert on `severity_bucket` in (critical, high) with per-`adaptor_mo` throttling, and hand the runbook to the compute compliance lead and SOAR queue named in detailedImplementation Step 2.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731) on a Heavy Forwarder or other node permitted to run modular inputs, plus a Search Head–visible copy of the same add-on (or a thin wrapper app) so props and lookup definitions resolve during interactive Search.
- Outbound `443/tcp` to the UCSM cluster or Fabric Interconnect management virtual IP, TLS trust for the server certificate chain, and a dedicated service user such as `splunk_ucs_inv` whose role includes read-only access to the Equipment–Inventory subtree that exposes `adaptorUnit` and the nested `firmwareRunning` objects under `.../adaptor-*/mgmt/`. Cisco documents an `aaa-monitor-priv` or equivalent read profile for API automation; align this account with a quarterly password rotation, not a personal admin account.
- A Splunk index (commonly `cisco_ucs`) and a role (for example `cisco_ucs_compliance`) whose `srchIndexesAllowed` list includes that index. Budget licence impact as roughly one to several events per slot per poll cycle for the adapter MO family—far smaller than per-second stat streams, but still proportional to the number of blades, rack units, and X-Series nodes under the same UCSM session.
- A private Splunk app (for example `ucs_adapter_fw_governance/`) to hold `lookups/ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv`, `lookups/ucs_maintenance_windows.csv`, and `default/transforms.conf` entries so you are not editing files under the vendor app’s `default/` tree. Version-control those CSVs in git with a peer-reviewed pull request for every baseline string change; attach the same commit hash to the change ticket the infrastructure board already uses for hardware bundles.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
(1) Install the add-on from Splunkbase 2731. On the collection host, copy the shipped `default/inputs.conf` to `local/inputs.conf` and enable the Cisco UCS inventory modular input whose vendor name matches the current release (stanza name varies by 4.2/4.3 packaging—mirror the `ciscoucs://` or `cisco_ucs://` pattern exactly from the sample block you receive with your build, not a hand-fabricated label). Set `disabled=0`, the UCSM URL, credentials, `index=cisco_ucs`, the documented poll `interval` (commonly 300–1800 seconds for inventory), and leave `sourcetype` at the add-on’s default `cisco:ucs:inventory` unless your organisation has a written exception process for sourcetype remaps.

(2) Extend the class list the modular input poller sends to the UCSM XML or JSON API so it includes, at minimum, `adaptorUnit` and `firmwareRunning`. Many deployments already pull `firmwareRunning` for UC-19.1.3; you must not drop the adapter path while trimming classes to save work. If your TA exposes a `stats_classes` or `inventory_classes` string, add the adapter-related identifiers exactly as the Cisco UCSM schema spells them. Optional: add `adaptorHostEthIf` and `adaptorHostFcIf` for cross-check queries that list how many vNICs or vHBAs are bound, but the compliance equality in the primary SPL keys off **adaptorUnit** and **firmwareRunning** only, because the host-interface MOs do not replace the on-card system image for mLOM, mezz, and PCIe VICs such as 1225, 1227, 1227T, 1240, 1280, 1285, 1340, 1380, 1387, 1440, 1455, 1457, 1480, 1485, 1495, 1497, and the X-series VIC 14425, 15235, 15422, 15428 families on rear mezz and mLOM slots.

(3) Example `local/inputs.conf` shape (field names are illustrative—always reconcile with your TA’s `default/inputs.conf` after install and adjust keys like `class_filter`, `inventory_classes`, or the stanza’s actual parameter names):

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf
# Heavy Forwarder — read-only service account, TLS trust to the UCSM VIP

#[ciscoucs://<copy_vendor_stanza_name_from_default>]
#disabled = 0
#url = https://<ucsm-vip-or-fi-cluster-vip>
#index = cisco_ucs
#interval = 600
# ... credential reference per vendor UI ...
# Ensure the inventory or class list includes: adaptorUnit, firmwareRunning
# (and optionally adaptorHostEthIf, adaptorHostFcIf for follow-on audits)
```

(4) `props.conf` in your wrapper app can quiet noisy line merging on JSON or XML and normalise time zones; most teams only need a lightweight stanza that references the TA’s existing extraction, for example: `TRUNCATE = 0` on `cisco:ucs:inventory` if firmware strings were clipped after an upgrade, and a consistent `MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD` for long adapter part numbers. Use `btool` on the forwarder to confirm your local stanza wins: `splunk btool props list cisco:ucs:inventory --debug | head -50`.

(5) Author `lookups/ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv` with UTF-8 encoding and a header that matches the `lookup` command in the primary SPL, for example: `adapter_model,approved_fw,bundle_id,lcm_phase` where `adapter_model` is the `model` string you observe on **adaptorUnit** events (for example `UCSB-VIC-1340`, `UCS-VIC-1480`, `UCSB-MLOM-40G-04`), `approved_fw` is the exact `running` / `firmwareRunning.version` value your infrastructure board adopted from the current Cisco HCL bundle release notes, `bundle_id` is a human key such as `4.2(3l)-infra-2025Q4`, and `lcm_phase` is an optional `prod` / `pilot` / `deferred` label your governance team can filter on. Populate rows by exporting the “approved infrastructure bundle” table your compute architects maintain for each B-series, C-series, and X-series wave, and copy the VIC, mLOM, or mezz line items from the same PDF or bundle spreadsheet where you already read BIOS and CIMC—this UC simply tracks the **adapter** line separately. When a single part number is approved as a range, either split multiple rows (preferred for string equality) or add a `notes` field and a future macro that rewrites comparisons—do not leave silent wildcards in the first pass.

In the same app, create `default/transforms.conf` (or `local/transforms.conf`) with at least:

```
[ucs_adapter_fw_baseline]
filename = ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 1
```

…then `lookup ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv` in SPL resolves. Reload the search head or deploy the shcluster bundle after edits.

(6) Optional `lookups/ucs_maintenance_windows.csv` with `server_mo,in_maintenance,maintenance_note` lets you filter planned FI or chassis work that is meant to look like drift. Define `[ucs_maintenance_windows]` in the same `transforms.conf` as the baseline lookup, `reload deploy-server` if this sits on a deployment server, and `splunk _internal` tail if you are debugging lookup permissions.

(7) Expected event volume: one inventory pass typically emits one or more `cisco:ucs:inventory` events per class per managed object, so a two-FI domain with 200 server slots and two adapters on average is on the order of a few hundred adapter-related events per hour at a 10–15 minute interval—small compared to stats streams but still subject to your retention policy. If you also enable Intersight for IMM nodes, that traffic lands in a different index; keep queries separate to avoid double-counting the same card.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**
Paste the same SPL you saved in the top-level `spl` field. It operates in five conceptual stages after the base `index`/`sourcetype`/`earliest` filter:

```spl
`comment("UC-19.1.32 — CNA / vNIC Adapter Firmware Drift (Cisco UCS): per-slot mLOM, mezz, and PCIe VIC firmware vs ucs_adapter_fw_baseline. Uses classId=adaptorUnit and firmwareRunning on cisco:ucs:inventory, not object_type=adapter.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-7d@d
| eval class_id=lower(coalesce('classId', class_id, ""))
| where class_id IN ("adaptorunit", "firmwarerunning")
| eval raw_dn=coalesce('dn', "")
| eval adaptor_mo=if(match(raw_dn, "/(adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]"), replace(raw_dn, "^(.*\/(?:adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]+).*$", "\1"), null())
| eval is_unit=if(class_id="adaptorunit", 1, 0)
| eval is_fwr=if(class_id="firmwarerunning", 1, 0)
| eval pres=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('presence', presence, ""), null())
| eval model0=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('model', model, adapter_model, 'partNumber', partNumber, ""), null())
| eval run_u=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('running', runningVersion, 'runningVersion', ""), null())
| eval start_u=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('startup', startupVersion, 'startupVersion', ""), null())
| eval fwr_dep=lower(trim(coalesce('deployment', deployment, "")))
| eval fwr_type=lower(trim(coalesce('type', type, "system")))
| eval fwr_run=if(is_fwr=1 AND fwr_dep="running" AND fwr_type IN ("system", "combined", ""), coalesce('version', version, ""), null())
| eval fwr_start=if(is_fwr=1 AND fwr_dep="startup" AND fwr_type IN ("system", "combined", ""), coalesce('version', version, ""), null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen, max(is_unit) as has_unit, max(pres) as presence, max(model0) as adapter_model, max(run_u) as run_unit, max(start_u) as start_unit, max(fwr_run) as run_mo, max(fwr_start) as start_mo by adaptor_mo
| where has_unit=1 AND presence!="missing" AND isnotnull(adaptor_mo) AND adaptor_mo!=""
| eval running_fw=coalesce(nullif(run_mo,""), nullif(run_unit,""), "")
| eval startup_fw=if(isnotnull(start_unit) AND start_unit!="", start_unit, if(isnotnull(start_mo) AND start_mo!="", start_mo, running_fw))
| eval server_mo=replace(adaptor_mo, "/(adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]+$", "")
| rex field=adaptor_mo "(?i).*(?:adaptor|adapter)-(?<slot>[0-9]+)$"
| fillnull value="unknown" slot
| eval norm_run=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(running_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| eval norm_start=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(startup_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| lookup ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv adapter_model OUTPUT approved_fw, bundle_id, lcm_phase
| eval norm_approved=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(approved_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| where isnotnull(approved_fw) AND approved_fw!="" AND (norm_run!=norm_approved OR norm_start!=norm_approved)
| lookup local=true ucs_maintenance_windows.csv server_mo OUTPUT in_maintenance, maintenance_note
| where in_maintenance!="1" OR isnull(in_maintenance)
| eval business_org=case(
    match(server_mo, "(org-[^/]+/|/ls-)") OR match(server_mo, "org-root/org-prod/"), "prod",
    match(server_mo, "/org-qa/|org-root/org-qa/|/qa-"), "qa",
    (isnotnull(lcm_phase) AND match(lcm_phase, "[Pp]rod")), "prod", true(), "other")
| eval drift_flavor=case(
    norm_run!=norm_approved AND norm_start!=norm_approved, "running_and_startup_drift",
    norm_run=norm_approved AND norm_start!=norm_approved, "startup_drift_only",
    norm_run!=norm_approved AND norm_start=norm_approved, "fw_staged_pending_reboot", true(), "mixed")
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    drift_flavor="running_and_startup_drift" AND business_org="prod", "critical",
    drift_flavor="running_and_startup_drift" AND business_org!="prod", "high",
    drift_flavor="startup_drift_only" AND business_org="prod", "medium",
    drift_flavor="fw_staged_pending_reboot" AND business_org="prod", "medium",
    drift_flavor="startup_drift_only", "low",
    drift_flavor="fw_staged_pending_reboot", "info", true(), "low")
| where severity_bucket IN ("critical", "high", "medium")
| table last_seen, server_mo, adaptor_mo, slot, adapter_model, running_fw, startup_fw, approved_fw, bundle_id, business_org, drift_flavor, severity_bucket, norm_run, norm_start
```


First, it lowercases the UCS **classId** and keeps only `adaptorUnit` and `firmwareRunning` events. That replaces anti-patterns that assumed an `object_type=adapter` field your TA may never set.

Second, it builds `adaptor_mo` as the full distinguished name up to the `.../adaptor-N` (or `adapter-N`) anchor, which works for B-series `sys/chassis-*/blade-*` paths, C-series `sys/rack-unit-*` paths, and the X-series shapes your estate uses. All firmware rows that share the same parent adapter collapse into a single `stats` row.

Third, the `stats` pass merges `running`/`startup` attributes from the **adaptorUnit** object with the `version` from **firmwareRunning** rows filtered to `deployment=running` and `type` in `system` or `combined`, and picks up a separate startup bank when the MO exposes it. The `where` clause drops missing adapters, empty DNs, and `presence=missing` slots where a card was pulled and should not page anyone.

Fourth, a normalisation `eval` maps parentheses-style Cisco versions to a dot-normalised lowercase token so you can compare `5.3(1.4)` workbooks against `5.3.1.4` if your CSV used an alternate print style. The `lookup` pulls `approved_fw` and the governance labels from `ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv`. The `ucs_maintenance_windows` join suppresses rows during CAB-approved work.

Fifth, after `norm_approved` is compared to `norm_run` and `norm_start`, `drift_flavor` and `severity_bucket` classify both-bank drift, startup-only drift, and running-only drift (often a staged activation pending reboot), and the final `where` keeps alertable severities. The closing `table` includes `last_seen` from the inventory poll so you can tell stale polls from ongoing drift.

Save as a scheduled report `uc_19_1_32_vic_fw_drift` with `cron` daily at 06:00 local, `earliest=-7d@d`, and an alert on `severity_bucket` in ("critical","high") for tickets. Throttle the alert to one event per 24 hours per `adaptor_mo` so a legitimate multi-day drift does not re-page daily until acknowledged. Map alert actions to your SOAR or ITSM (for example ServiceNow connector) and name the runbook owner `UCS Firmware Compliance Lead` with a backup delegate on the `Platform Compute – Cisco` rotation. The SOAR play should create a child task to verify activation windows under **Settings** in the UCSM firmware workflow when `drift_flavor` suggests staged firmware.

**Step 3 — Validate**
Pick one adapter row the search marked as `critical` or `high`, copy `adaptor_mo` and `server_mo` into a scratch notepad, then open the UCSM user interface. Navigate **Equipment** → the chassis or **Rack Units** path that matches the left side of the DN, open the **Server** node, then **Inventory** → **Adapters** (or the **Inventory** tab on the **Servers** list that expands adapters). Expand the adapter that shares the `adaptor` index from the Splunk `slot` field and read **Running Version** and **Backup Version** (or **Startup** depending on 4.2/4.3 wording). The strings must match the `running_fw` and `startup_fw` fields after accounting for the vendor’s display shortening—if UCSM shortens a build token, copy the long form from the **Firmware Management** view instead.

Next, for bundle truth, go to **Admin** → **Firmware Management** (or **Equipment** → **Firmware Management** in some builds) → **Firmware Auto Install** and read **Bundle Currently Installed** and the infrastructure bundle name. Compare that to your `bundle_id` column. If the bundle on the server is different from the global domain repository because of a per-service-profile override, you must reflect that in the CSV for that `adapter_model` or add a per-org column and extend the SPL to join it.

For IMM-only hosts, open **Intersight** in the cloud tenant, go to **Servers** → the device → **Inventory** and review **Adapter Cards** on the device detail; compare that card’s `adapter.Unit` running image to the value you get from the Intersight add-on, not the UCSM string, or redirect this validation to UC-19.1.20 when that is the only authoritative record.

**Step 4 — Operationalize**
Publish a “UCS VIC and CNA firmware posture” dashboard with: row 1, single value tiles for `critical`, `high`, and `medium` counts; row 2, the heatmap of `adapter_model` versus the observed `running_fw` token; row 3, the wide table the SPL already emits; row 4, a filter-only panel showing org from `lcm_phase` or a CMDB join if you add one later. Tie weekly saved-search output to the same change-advisory body that approves Cisco infrastructure bundles, and add a **quarterly** adapter-firmware review that is shorter than a full re-certification of every OS in UC-19.1.21 but still re-reads Cisco PSIRT notes for the exact VIC families in your data hall. For PSIRTs that name a specific **adapter** class, add a `cve_risk` column to the CSV in a future release and join it like UC-19.1.20’s `cve_count` idea without copying that UC wholesale.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
If the base search returns zero rows while you know a slot is out of date, first confirm events exist: `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-1d | stats count by classId` must show `adaptorUnit` and `firmwareRunning` with non-zero counts. If the adapter class is zero, the modular input’s class list is still trimmed—return to Step 1 and re-enable the MO.

If events exist but the join produces empty `adaptor_mo`, your DN pattern may include uppercase `Adaptor` in older UCSM—extend the `replace` in the SPL with a case-insensitive `rex` and retest. If the baseline lookup never hits, `adapter_model` from UCS may include trailing spaces: `| eval adapter_model=trim(adapter_model)` before the `lookup`.

If `norm_run` always equals the lookup yet UCSM still shows a mismatch, you likely copied a shortened marketing label into the CSV—open one raw `adaptorUnit` with `| head 1` and read all keys with `| fieldsummary` to capture the true key names; UCSM 4.1, 4.2, and 4.3 sometimes emit `running` vs `runningVersion` only on one of the two objects.

If noise spikes during a known bundle wave, re-use your maintenance file or query `cisco:ucs:inventory` for `firmwareCompSource` or other firmware-source MOs your TA exposes and correlate a rising event count to the same window as `ucs_maintenance_windows`. If only the startup bank is wrong while the running bank is approved, treat that as a pending activation problem and adjust severities in the `eval` rather than silencing the entire UC.

For IMM stand-alone, switch the primary index to `cisco_intersight` and the sourcetype the adapter `adapter.Unit` object uses (`cisco:intersight:adapterunit`), and replace `adaptor_mo` with the device plus slot key your TA encodes, or run two saved searches. Finally, if everything is null, confirm the Heavy Forwarder’s clock and that the UCSM service account is not scoped to a child org that omits the adapter subtree—GUI admins with broader org rights than the API user will otherwise show attachments Splunk never polls.


## SPL

```spl
`comment("UC-19.1.32 — CNA / vNIC Adapter Firmware Drift (Cisco UCS): per-slot mLOM, mezz, and PCIe VIC firmware vs ucs_adapter_fw_baseline. Uses classId=adaptorUnit and firmwareRunning on cisco:ucs:inventory, not object_type=adapter.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-7d@d
| eval class_id=lower(coalesce('classId', class_id, ""))
| where class_id IN ("adaptorunit", "firmwarerunning")
| eval raw_dn=coalesce('dn', "")
| eval adaptor_mo=if(match(raw_dn, "/(adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]"), replace(raw_dn, "^(.*\/(?:adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]+).*$", "\1"), null())
| eval is_unit=if(class_id="adaptorunit", 1, 0)
| eval is_fwr=if(class_id="firmwarerunning", 1, 0)
| eval pres=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('presence', presence, ""), null())
| eval model0=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('model', model, adapter_model, 'partNumber', partNumber, ""), null())
| eval run_u=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('running', runningVersion, 'runningVersion', ""), null())
| eval start_u=if(is_unit=1, coalesce('startup', startupVersion, 'startupVersion', ""), null())
| eval fwr_dep=lower(trim(coalesce('deployment', deployment, "")))
| eval fwr_type=lower(trim(coalesce('type', type, "system")))
| eval fwr_run=if(is_fwr=1 AND fwr_dep="running" AND fwr_type IN ("system", "combined", ""), coalesce('version', version, ""), null())
| eval fwr_start=if(is_fwr=1 AND fwr_dep="startup" AND fwr_type IN ("system", "combined", ""), coalesce('version', version, ""), null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen, max(is_unit) as has_unit, max(pres) as presence, max(model0) as adapter_model, max(run_u) as run_unit, max(start_u) as start_unit, max(fwr_run) as run_mo, max(fwr_start) as start_mo by adaptor_mo
| where has_unit=1 AND presence!="missing" AND isnotnull(adaptor_mo) AND adaptor_mo!=""
| eval running_fw=coalesce(nullif(run_mo,""), nullif(run_unit,""), "")
| eval startup_fw=if(isnotnull(start_unit) AND start_unit!="", start_unit, if(isnotnull(start_mo) AND start_mo!="", start_mo, running_fw))
| eval server_mo=replace(adaptor_mo, "/(adaptor|adapter)-[0-9]+$", "")
| rex field=adaptor_mo "(?i).*(?:adaptor|adapter)-(?<slot>[0-9]+)$"
| fillnull value="unknown" slot
| eval norm_run=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(running_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| eval norm_start=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(startup_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| lookup ucs_adapter_fw_baseline.csv adapter_model OUTPUT approved_fw, bundle_id, lcm_phase
| eval norm_approved=lower(trim(replace(replace(replace(approved_fw, " ", ""), "(", "."), ")", "")))
| where isnotnull(approved_fw) AND approved_fw!="" AND (norm_run!=norm_approved OR norm_start!=norm_approved)
| lookup local=true ucs_maintenance_windows.csv server_mo OUTPUT in_maintenance, maintenance_note
| where in_maintenance!="1" OR isnull(in_maintenance)
| eval business_org=case(
    match(server_mo, "(org-[^/]+/|/ls-)") OR match(server_mo, "org-root/org-prod/"), "prod",
    match(server_mo, "/org-qa/|org-root/org-qa/|/qa-"), "qa",
    (isnotnull(lcm_phase) AND match(lcm_phase, "[Pp]rod")), "prod", true(), "other")
| eval drift_flavor=case(
    norm_run!=norm_approved AND norm_start!=norm_approved, "running_and_startup_drift",
    norm_run=norm_approved AND norm_start!=norm_approved, "startup_drift_only",
    norm_run!=norm_approved AND norm_start=norm_approved, "fw_staged_pending_reboot", true(), "mixed")
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    drift_flavor="running_and_startup_drift" AND business_org="prod", "critical",
    drift_flavor="running_and_startup_drift" AND business_org!="prod", "high",
    drift_flavor="startup_drift_only" AND business_org="prod", "medium",
    drift_flavor="fw_staged_pending_reboot" AND business_org="prod", "medium",
    drift_flavor="startup_drift_only", "low",
    drift_flavor="fw_staged_pending_reboot", "info", true(), "low")
| where severity_bucket IN ("critical", "high", "medium")
| table last_seen, server_mo, adaptor_mo, slot, adapter_model, running_fw, startup_fw, approved_fw, bundle_id, business_org, drift_flavor, severity_bucket, norm_run, norm_start
```

## Visualization

Heatmap of `adapter_model` (rows) and observed `running_fw` (columns) to spot surprise clusters. Sortable table of `server_mo`, `adaptor_mo`, `slot`, `adapter_model`, `running_fw`, `startup_fw`, `approved_fw`, `drift_flavor`, `severity_bucket` for the engineering queue. Single-value: count of `critical` plus `high` in the lookback. Optional: overlay `bundle_id` as a small-multiples column when multiple golden bundles coexist across orgs. Side panel with UC-19.1.3 for server-level BIOS and CIMC so reviewers see the adapter line item side by side during bundle upgrades.

## Known False Positives

During a rolling **UCSM bundle** upgrade, the management plane and server CIMC are often updated before the **adaptor** images finish activation; you will see a window where the adapter is still on the previous component build even though the domain is not broken. A replacement card that came from a cold-spare RMA with factory firmware and is intentionally aligned on the next maintenance day will also appear as drift until you schedule activation or refresh the **ucs_adapter_fw_baseline** row with an approved interim build written in your change record. C-Series nodes where an operator side-loaded a Cisco field hot-fix image outside the current bundle, with explicit platform-team approval, will trip until you either add an exception row keyed on serial or widen the `approved_fw` string to that hot-fix label. X-Series mLOM or rear mezz work staged for a firmware activation during the same cutover that already shows **fw_staged_pending_reboot** on the running bank can look harsher than reality—throttle to `adaptor_mo` and cross-check the UCSM firmware auto-install or pending-activation FSM, not a single 15 minute poll. Lab orgs and intentionally lagging qualification blades should be down-tiered with an org-based `business_org` map or a nonprod column in the baseline CSV, not the same sev-1 path as a production org.
